news_summary: Advancements in clean energy and habitat restoration are driving positive environmental progress, with nuclear fusion nearing grid integration, solar farms supporting wildlife on restored peatlands, and water voles returning to reserves after decades.
news_summary_fr: Les avancées dans le domaine des énergies propres et de la restauration des habitats contribuent à des progrès environnementaux significatifs : la fusion nucléaire est sur le point d'être intégrée au réseau électrique, les parcs solaires favorisent la faune sauvage sur les tourbières restaurées, et les campagnols aquatiques reviennent dans les réserves après des décennies d'absence.
news_summary_es: Los avances en materia de energía limpia y restauración de hábitats están impulsando un progreso medioambiental positivo: la fusión nuclear está a punto de integrarse en la red eléctrica, los parques solares contribuyen a la conservación de la fauna silvestre en turberas restauradas y las ratas de agua regresan a las reservas tras décadas de ausencia.

articles.title: Nuclear-fusion firm says plant will deliver electricity to grid – but big questions remain
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 08 June 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01795-zCommonwealth Fusion Systems published several papers detailing the design of its ARC fusion power plant.
articles.content:
An illustration of a tokamak nuclear fusion reactor chamber.Credit: Ruslanas Baranauskas/SPL

One of the world’s leading private fusion companies, Commonwealth Fusion Systems (CFS), has published a suite of papers that the firm says “confirm” that its ARC power plant, if built as intended, will produce more electricity than it consumes. But some researchers say that results from an operational fusion reactor are needed to validate their predictions and that big engineering challenges remain to be solved.

Private fusion firms have received almost US$10 billion of investments over the past decade, with the promise that fusion — the reaction that powers the Sun — could be harnessed on Earth to produce clean electricity. CFS, which is based in Cambridge, Massachusetts, and other firms including Helion Energy in Everett, Washington, and TAE Technologies in Foothill Ranch, California, say that they will deliver commercial fusion plants by the early 2030s.

Physicists at the US National Ignition Facility in Livermore, California, created the first fusion reaction that briefly produced more energy than it consumed in 2022. But no team has made a reactor that can produce energy continuously, or enough to leave a surplus, or proved that a reactor can be run in an economically viable way.

US nuclear-fusion lab enters new era: achieving ‘ignition’ over and over

One paper was published on 14 April and another four were published on 4 June in the Journal of Plasma Physics1–5. The articles were authored by 58 researchers from CFS and its academic partner institutes. The authors lay out the design of the ARC power plant and the physics behind it — including modelling the expected behaviour of plasma, the superhot matter in which fusion takes place. The researchers emphasize uncertainties that must be addressed before the design is finalized.

But, speaking to journalists at a briefing ahead of the papers’ publication, Brandon Sorbom — CFS’ chief science officer based in Devens, Massachusetts — seemed confident. He said the papers “confirm that when we build the ARC fusion power plant, it will work”.

The team behind it are “some of the best in the fusion business” and their concept is based on “good work over many years”, says Tony Roulstone, a nuclear engineer at the University of Cambridge, UK. But because the company is funded by private capital “there is pressure to claim things before the evidence is fully in place”, he says.

Workers at the Commonwealth Fusion Systems magnet factory.Credit: Commonwealth Fusion Systems

Plasma doughnut

CFS was founded as a spin-off company from the Massachusetts Institute of Technology in Cambridge in 2018. The company has since raised almost $3 billion and is gearing up to begin operations of its prototype, a demonstration machine known as SPARC, next year. Results from SPARC will be used to hone much of the ARC power plant’s design, which could begin operations in the early 2030s.

The company says that SPARC will demonstrate net energy, that is, produce more energy than it needs to confine and heat the plasma, but not necessarily more than the wider plant uses to run. Its successor, ARC, should produce enough energy to not only sustain plant operations but also to continuously deliver 400 megawatts of net electricity to the grid, an amount that could power around 280,000 average US homes, says the firm.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a significant advancement in nuclear fusion technology by Commonwealth Fusion Systems, which aims to produce clean, net-positive electricity with their ARC power plant. This development promises a large-scale, sustainable energy source that could benefit society broadly by providing clean electricity to hundreds of thousands of homes. Although challenges remain and operational validation is pending, the detailed design papers and upcoming prototype testing represent meaningful progress toward a major breakthrough in renewable energy.
articles.personality_title: Fusion company plans to deliver clean electricity with new power plant
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Nuclear fusion is the process that powers the Sun, and scientists have long hoped to use it on Earth to create clean energy. Private companies have invested billions to develop fusion reactors that can produce more electricity than they use, but no one has yet built a reactor that works continuously and efficiently.

**What happened** – Commonwealth Fusion Systems (CFS), a company from Massachusetts, published detailed plans for its ARC fusion power plant. This plant is designed to create more electricity than it consumes and deliver 400 megawatts of power to the electric grid, enough for about 280,000 homes. CFS will soon test a smaller prototype called SPARC to check their ideas before building ARC.

**Impact** – If successful, ARC would be one of the first fusion power plants to provide clean, net-positive electricity on a large scale. This could change how we produce energy by reducing reliance on fossil fuels. The detailed design papers show careful research, but experts say real tests with the prototype are still needed to prove the technology works as planned.

**What's next step** – CFS plans to start operating the SPARC prototype next year. The results from SPARC will help improve ARC’s design. If SPARC works well, construction of the full ARC plant could begin, aiming to supply electricity in the early 2030s.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Commonwealth Fusion Systems is moving closer to building a fusion power plant that could provide clean electricity to hundreds of thousands of homes, but real-world testing is still needed to confirm its success.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une entreprise de fusion prévoit de fournir de l’électricité propre avec une nouvelle centrale
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La fusion nucléaire est le processus qui alimente le Soleil, et les scientifiques espèrent depuis longtemps l’utiliser sur Terre pour produire de l’énergie propre. Des entreprises privées ont investi des milliards pour développer des réacteurs à fusion capables de produire plus d’électricité qu’ils n’en consomment, mais personne n’a encore construit un réacteur fonctionnant en continu et efficacement.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Commonwealth Fusion Systems (CFS), une entreprise du Massachusetts, a publié des plans détaillés pour sa centrale à fusion ARC. Cette centrale est conçue pour créer plus d’électricité qu’elle n’en consomme et fournir 400 mégawatts au réseau électrique, soit assez pour environ 280 000 foyers. CFS va bientôt tester un prototype plus petit appelé SPARC pour vérifier leurs idées avant de construire ARC.

**Impact** – Si elle réussit, ARC serait l’une des premières centrales à fusion à fournir de l’électricité propre et positive à grande échelle. Cela pourrait changer la production d’énergie en réduisant la dépendance aux énergies fossiles. Les articles détaillés montrent une recherche sérieuse, mais des experts soulignent que des tests réels avec le prototype sont encore nécessaires pour prouver que la technologie fonctionne comme prévu.

**Prochaine étape** – CFS prévoit de commencer à faire fonctionner le prototype SPARC l’année prochaine. Les résultats de SPARC aideront à améliorer la conception d’ARC. Si SPARC fonctionne bien, la construction de la centrale ARC pourrait commencer, avec un objectif pour le début des années 2030.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Commonwealth Fusion Systems se rapproche de la construction d’une centrale à fusion capable de fournir de l’électricité propre à des centaines de milliers de foyers, mais des tests concrets restent nécessaires pour confirmer son succès.
articles.personality_title_es: Empresa de fusión planea entregar electricidad limpia con nueva planta
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La fusión nuclear es el proceso que alimenta al Sol, y los científicos han esperado durante mucho tiempo usarla en la Tierra para crear energía limpia. Empresas privadas han invertido miles de millones para desarrollar reactores de fusión que produzcan más electricidad de la que consumen, pero nadie ha construido aún un reactor que funcione de manera continua y eficiente.

**Qué pasó** – Commonwealth Fusion Systems (CFS), una empresa de Massachusetts, publicó planes detallados para su planta de fusión ARC. Esta planta está diseñada para generar más electricidad de la que usa y entregar 400 megavatios al sistema eléctrico, suficiente para unas 280,000 casas. CFS probará pronto un prototipo más pequeño llamado SPARC para verificar sus ideas antes de construir ARC.

**Impacto** – Si tiene éxito, ARC sería una de las primeras plantas de fusión que provea electricidad limpia y neta a gran escala. Esto podría cambiar la forma en que producimos energía al reducir la dependencia de combustibles fósiles. Los documentos detallados muestran una investigación cuidadosa, pero expertos dicen que aún se necesitan pruebas reales con el prototipo para confirmar que la tecnología funciona.

**Próximo paso** – CFS planea comenzar a operar el prototipo SPARC el próximo año. Los resultados de SPARC ayudarán a mejorar el diseño de ARC. Si SPARC funciona bien, la construcción de la planta ARC podría comenzar, con la meta de suministrar electricidad a principios de la década de 2030.

**Resumen en una frase** – Commonwealth Fusion Systems avanza hacia la construcción de una planta de fusión que podría proporcionar electricidad limpia a cientos de miles de hogares, aunque aún se necesitan pruebas en el mundo real para confirmar su éxito.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a glowing tokamak fusion reactor chamber shaped like a luminous plasma doughnut, suspended above a stylized electrical grid symbolized by interconnected glowing lines, with soft natural hues of blue, orange, and gold highlighting the energy flow and futuristic technology in harmony with a calm, abstract background suggesting innovation and hope.

articles.title: Wildlife thrives in solar farm built on restored peatland
articles.summary: A diverse range of bird species has been recorded at a solar park on rewetted peatland in Germany, suggesting that combining energy generation with habitat restoration could benefit biodiversity, the climate and the economy
articles.content:
A diverse range of bird species has been recorded at a solar park on rewetted peatland in Germany, suggesting that combining energy generation with habitat restoration could benefit biodiversity, the climate and the economy

A meadow pipit at the solar park on peatland in northern Germany Wattmanufactur

A solar farm on a rewetted peatland is home to more types of bird than drained agricultural fields nearby, suggesting that land used for renewable energy can make money for landowners, lock away carbon and boost biodiversity at the same time.

Peatlands are the largest terrestrial carbon store, holding twice as much carbon as all forests. But enormous tracts of them have been drained to create farm fields or dug up to produce potting soil for gardening. Ninety-five per cent of peatlands in Germany and 80 per cent in the UK have been degraded.

Advertisement

Once farmers drain a peatland with ditches and pumps, microbes begin breaking down the ancient carbon it holds, emitting carbon dioxide for decades or centuries.

A German state-funded research programme has begun looking at whether solar farms could help promote peatland restoration.

“We can’t simply just say, ‘Let’s just rewet it all and put it back to nature conservation’,” says Hanna Rae Martens at the University of Griefswald, Germany, who worked on the research. “There’s a lot of people on the land who are looking for income.”

Free newsletter Sign up to The Earth Edition Unmissable news about our planet, delivered straight to your inbox each month. Sign up to newsletter

At the study site, the solar company Wattmanufactur started building sand-and-gravel roads in 2020 that cut off the flow of water to existing drainage ditches, allowing the farm field to accumulate water and turn back into peatland.

The study was the first to look at the impacts of putting solar on rewetted peatland, and it found a benefit for biodiversity, according to Martens.

“A fear here is we destroy habitat, and in this case, it is not the case,” she says. “There’s habitat created for some species, and it’s being created for some endangered and some wetland species, and it has the potential to contribute to landscape-level diversity.”

Although species richness was about the same in the 30-hectare solar park and in two fields nearby that were regularly mown for hay, audio recorders showed the solar park had both wetland and woodland bird species, while the hay fields had only grassland species like the European goldfinch.

White wagtails, reed buntings and grey herons, all wetland species, were registered in the solar park, but so were woodland species like tree pipits and Eurasian tree sparrows. The solar panels seemed to be standing in for shrubs and small trees, with birds like buzzards and kestrels perching on them to hunt mice in the grass below.

Researchers also photographed the meadow pipit, a small, brown-streaked grassland species that is threatened in Germany, on the panels.

Martens thinks the rewetted peat, the solar panels for perching and the reduction in grass mowing all helped attract birds. But further research is needed to see how that biodiversity compares with that of rewetted peatland without solar panels, according to Catherine Waite at the University of Cambridge.

“Peatland PV [photovoltaics]… could be a really good way to kind of help regenerate heavily degraded agricultural peat, but that doesn’t mean it would also be a good thing to put it on really healthy peatlands elsewhere,” she says.

While the UK has restored 2500 square kilometres of peatland, that is only one-tenth of what has been damaged, and Germany has restored far less. Moreover, because the emissions from drained peat continue for many years, some of the 165 solar farms that have been put on degraded peatlands in Germany are actually releasing more greenhouse gases than they are displacing with their carbon-free energy.

Unlike agrovoltaics, where grazing or crop-growing continues around the solar panels, peatland PV doesn’t currently generate income beyond electricity sales. The Wattmanufactur solar park is one of only five on rewetted peatland. Solar developers on peatland often have to install deeper posts and wait until the dry summer period before starting construction, increasing costs.

Although Germany has barred solar farms on degraded peatland from receiving a minimum guaranteed electricity price since 2023, developers aren’t always forced to declare if their installation is on drained peat.

More government incentives are likely to be needed for peatland PV to really grow, says Waite. “If we want to deal with things like global warming and the biodiversity crisis, but also how to feed people, we have to manage land in a way that provides multiple benefits,” she says. “So we have to have these win-wins.”
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a solar farm built on restored peatland in Germany that benefits biodiversity by supporting diverse bird species, contributes to climate action by locking away carbon, and offers economic benefits. This represents a meaningful, tangible environmental breakthrough combining renewable energy generation with habitat restoration, addressing climate and biodiversity crises on a significant scale.
articles.personality_title: Solar farm on restored peatland in Germany boosts bird life and climate benefits
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Peatlands are wet areas that store a lot of carbon, helping to slow climate change. But many peatlands in Germany and the UK have been drained to make farmland, which releases carbon into the air and harms wildlife.

**What happened** – In northern Germany, a solar farm was built on peatland that was restored by blocking drainage ditches to bring back water. This started in 2020. Researchers found that this solar farm supports more kinds of birds, including wetland and woodland species, compared to nearby farm fields.

**Impact** – This is important because it shows solar farms can produce clean energy while helping nature recover. The solar panels provide places for birds to perch, and the wetter land attracts species that had disappeared. This method can protect wildlife, reduce carbon emissions, and give farmers income from energy.

**What's next step** – Scientists want to study more places like this to understand how solar farms on restored peatlands compare to peatlands without solar panels. Governments may need to offer more support to encourage this approach, as building on peatland can be costly and tricky.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Combining solar energy with peatland restoration in Germany is helping to protect birds and fight climate change at the same time.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une centrale solaire sur tourbière restaurée en Allemagne favorise la biodiversité et le climat
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les tourbières sont des zones humides qui stockent beaucoup de carbone, aidant à ralentir le changement climatique. Mais beaucoup de tourbières en Allemagne et au Royaume-Uni ont été drainées pour l'agriculture, ce qui libère du carbone dans l'air et nuit à la faune.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Dans le nord de l'Allemagne, une centrale solaire a été construite sur une tourbière restaurée en bloquant les drains pour faire remonter l'eau. Cela a commencé en 2020. Les chercheurs ont découvert que cette centrale abrite plus d'espèces d'oiseaux, y compris des espèces de zones humides et forestières, comparé aux champs agricoles voisins.

**Impact** – C'est important car cela montre que les centrales solaires peuvent produire de l'énergie propre tout en aidant la nature à se rétablir. Les panneaux solaires servent de perchoirs aux oiseaux, et la terre humide attire des espèces disparues. Cette méthode protège la faune, réduit les émissions de carbone et offre un revenu aux agriculteurs.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques veulent étudier davantage d'endroits similaires pour comprendre comment les centrales solaires sur tourbières restaurées se comparent aux tourbières sans panneaux solaires. Les gouvernements pourraient devoir offrir plus de soutien car construire sur tourbière peut être coûteux et compliqué.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Combiner énergie solaire et restauration des tourbières en Allemagne aide à protéger les oiseaux et à lutter contre le changement climatique en même temps.
articles.personality_title_es: Parque solar en turbera restaurada en Alemania impulsa la vida de aves y el clima
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las turberas son zonas húmedas que almacenan mucho carbono, ayudando a frenar el cambio climático. Pero muchas turberas en Alemania y el Reino Unido han sido drenadas para la agricultura, lo que libera carbono al aire y daña la vida silvestre.

**Qué pasó** – En el norte de Alemania, se construyó un parque solar sobre una turbera restaurada al bloquear zanjas para que el agua regresara. Esto comenzó en 2020. Los investigadores encontraron que este parque solar tiene más tipos de aves, incluyendo especies de humedales y bosques, en comparación con campos agrícolas cercanos.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque muestra que los parques solares pueden generar energía limpia mientras ayudan a la naturaleza a recuperarse. Los paneles solares sirven como posaderos para las aves, y la tierra más húmeda atrae especies que habían desaparecido. Este método protege la fauna, reduce emisiones de carbono y ofrece ingresos a los agricultores.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos quieren estudiar más lugares así para entender cómo los parques solares en turberas restauradas se comparan con turberas sin paneles solares. Los gobiernos podrían necesitar dar más apoyo porque construir en turberas puede ser costoso y complicado.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Combinar energía solar con restauración de turberas en Alemania está ayudando a proteger aves y combatir el cambio climático al mismo tiempo.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a gently rewetted peatland meadow in northern Germany, featuring diverse bird species like meadow pipits perched on sleek solar panels that rise above shallow water and lush grasses, with the solar panels symbolically blending into the habitat as natural perches, under soft natural tones of green, brown, and blue, capturing the harmony of renewable energy and thriving biodiversity.

articles.title: Water voles return to reserve after 40 years
articles.summary: The town council says the rodent has returned to Batford Springs after 40 years.
articles.content: Councillor Kirsti Wenn, Mayor of Harpenden, said: "It has been a real pleasure to watch this conservation project, from its infancy to the release of our new residents, and I can't wait to see how the water voles will help the town council continue to improve the environment and local ecosystem.
articles.reasoning: The article reports the successful return of water voles to a local reserve after 40 years, indicating a positive conservation achievement that benefits the local ecosystem and environment. This has meaningful ecological impact and reflects effective environmental restoration efforts.
articles.personality_title: Water voles return to Batford Springs after 40 years
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Water voles are small rodents that live near rivers and wetlands. They disappeared from Batford Springs, a nature reserve in Harpenden, about 40 years ago because of habitat loss and pollution.

**What happened** – Recently, water voles were successfully reintroduced to Batford Springs. This project was led by the town council, with support from local conservation groups. Councillor Kirsti Wenn, Mayor of Harpenden, expressed excitement about the release of these animals back into their natural home.

**Impact** – The return of water voles shows that efforts to restore the environment at Batford Springs are working. Water voles help keep local ecosystems healthy by eating plants and providing food for other animals. Their presence means the habitat is improving, which benefits many other plants and animals.

**What's next step** – The town council plans to keep monitoring the water voles to make sure they thrive. They will also continue working on improving the reserve’s environment so that the water voles and other wildlife can live there for many years.

**One-sentence takeaway** – After 40 years, water voles have come back to Batford Springs, marking a successful step in restoring the local environment.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les campagnols d'eau reviennent à Batford Springs après 40 ans
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les campagnols d'eau sont de petits rongeurs qui vivent près des rivières et des zones humides. Ils ont disparu de Batford Springs, une réserve naturelle à Harpenden, il y a environ 40 ans à cause de la perte d'habitat et de la pollution.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Récemment, les campagnols d'eau ont été réintroduits avec succès à Batford Springs. Ce projet a été mené par le conseil municipal avec le soutien des groupes locaux de conservation. La conseillère Kirsti Wenn, maire de Harpenden, a exprimé son enthousiasme pour la réintroduction de ces animaux dans leur habitat naturel.

**Impact** – Le retour des campagnols d'eau montre que les efforts pour restaurer l'environnement à Batford Springs fonctionnent. Les campagnols d'eau aident à maintenir les écosystèmes locaux en mangeant des plantes et en servant de nourriture à d'autres animaux. Leur présence signifie que l'habitat s'améliore, ce qui profite à de nombreuses autres plantes et animaux.

**Prochaine étape** – Le conseil municipal prévoit de continuer à surveiller les campagnols d'eau pour s'assurer qu'ils prospèrent. Ils continueront aussi à améliorer l'environnement de la réserve pour que les campagnols et d'autres animaux puissent y vivre longtemps.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Après 40 ans, les campagnols d'eau sont revenus à Batford Springs, marquant une étape réussie dans la restauration de l'environnement local.
articles.personality_title_es: Los topillos de agua regresan a Batford Springs tras 40 años
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los topillos de agua son pequeños roedores que viven cerca de ríos y humedales. Desaparecieron de Batford Springs, una reserva natural en Harpenden, hace unos 40 años debido a la pérdida de hábitat y la contaminación.

**Qué pasó** – Recientemente, los topillos de agua fueron reintroducidos con éxito en Batford Springs. Este proyecto fue liderado por el ayuntamiento, con apoyo de grupos locales de conservación. La concejala Kirsti Wenn, alcaldesa de Harpenden, expresó su emoción por la liberación de estos animales en su hogar natural.

**Impacto** – El regreso de los topillos de agua muestra que los esfuerzos para restaurar el medio ambiente en Batford Springs están funcionando. Los topillos ayudan a mantener saludables los ecosistemas locales al alimentarse de plantas y ser alimento para otros animales. Su presencia indica que el hábitat está mejorando, beneficiando a muchas otras plantas y animales.

**Próximo paso** – El ayuntamiento planea seguir monitoreando a los topillos para asegurar que prosperen. También continuarán trabajando en mejorar el entorno de la reserva para que los topillos y otras especies puedan vivir allí por muchos años.

**Resumen en una frase** – Tras 40 años, los topillos de agua han regresado a Batford Springs, marcando un paso exitoso en la restauración del entorno local.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a peaceful riverside reserve at dawn, featuring a small family of water voles emerging from lush riverbank reeds into calm water, with soft reflections and gentle ripples symbolizing new life and environmental renewal.

articles.title: Sustainability or dystopia? What past patterns tell us about where society is heading
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 08 June 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01821-0A new book applies principles of environmental tipping points to past societal transformations — and attempts to draw lessons about what the future holds today.
articles.content:
Tipping Out of Trouble: How Societies Transformed and How We Can Do So Again Marten Scheffer Cambridge Univ. Press (2026)

What connects the collapse of Late Bronze Age civilizations in the eastern Mediterranean some 3,000 years ago, the abolition of the transatlantic slave trade in the nineteenth century, the societal and economic reforms of the US progressive era in the 1890s to 1920s, and falling fertility rates around the world today?

According to complex-systems researcher Marten Scheffer in his book, Tipping Out of Trouble, they are all examples of tipping points. Current societies have a crucial, diminishing chance to learn from them how to tip the world towards a sustainable, rather than dystopian, future.

Catastrophic change looms as Earth nears climate ‘tipping points’, report says

Tipping points have received substantial attention lately in research, the media and books. The focus has been mainly on environmental tipping points, in which gradual changes in conditions, such as rising atmospheric temperatures, reach a point at which parts of the Earth system irreversibly shift to a fundamentally different state. Two books published last year, Earth-system scientist Tim Lenton’s Positive Tipping Points and Am Kipppunkt, written by German journalists Toralf Staud and Benjamin von Brackel, deal with such ‘negative’ tipping points, as well as positive societal ones that might counter them.

Scheffer’s focus is squarely on past societal transformations, and on seeking out similar dynamics that might be at play today. His starting point is what he calls ‘the trouble’, which includes not just the environmental crisis of accelerating climate change and loss of nature, but also a rise in social tensions and inequality. Scheffer links this societal crisis to neoliberal ideas proposing smaller governments and deregulated markets that have gained traction globally since the 1970s. Noting that a “fundamental shift of scale is needed” in efforts to tackle climate change and nature loss, he asks what we can learn from past transformations, because “humanity has tipped out of trouble many times before”.

Drivers of change

To explore this question, Scheffer takes readers on a tour of societal transformations — collapses of ancient civilizations, modern revolutions and reforms and more-recent shifts in norms, such as smoking bans. He is a knowledgeable guide and writes in a conversational tone, sharing excerpts from his extensive scientific career. He argues that the nature of all these changes means that tipping points are the “universal phenomenon” behind them. Once a system threshold is breached, changes become self-sustaining and cause a substantial shift in that system’s state.

Coral die-off marks Earth’s first climate ‘tipping point’, scientists say

The evidence Scheffer presents is often circumstantial, and he recognizes that it’s easy to oversimplify real-world dynamics. In most cases, the abruptness of the shift is central to his argument. But tipping dynamics are only one reason for shifts in complex systems; they can also be due to abrupt forcing factors, be reversible and even just be the product of random variability. The abruptness of some transformations is also debatable. For instance, after the United Kingdom formally abolished the transatlantic slave trade in 1807, it took the best part of a century for other nations to do the same and for the practice, as well as the forms of forced labour that replaced it, to end. Similarly, some evidence suggests that the Late Bronze Age collapse was a more piecemeal transition than was previously thought.

Scheffer also suggests that critical slowing down — a phenomenon in which the resilience of a system measurably decreases before a big shift — could be a ‘smoking gun’ for a transition becoming a tipping point. As an example, he discusses evidence of gradual changes in societal resilience that seem to precede the Ancestral Pueblo people mysteriously abandoning settlements in what is now the southwestern United States several times in the centuries up to 1300.

Cliff dwellings of the Ancestral Pueblo people were mysteriously abandoned several times.Credit: Peter Unger/Getty

Scheffer proposes many positive feedback loops that might have driven tipping points in this and other cases. But the presence of such effects is no guarantee of tipping dynamics, and many of the proposed feedback loops remain hypothetical. Moreover, in some situations, resilience can be lost without the system reaching a tipping point, and a tipping point can occur without early warning signs.

As the late evolutionary biologist Richard Lewontin observed, “it seems impossible to do science without metaphors”, but we must consider the problems such figures of speech might bring — especially when they add conceptual baggage that alter our expectations of reality. In the case of tipping points, the concept has acquired, for many people, a sense of inevitability as it has traversed from social to natural sciences and back again. Scheffer mirrors this view, describing societal tipping points as “accidents in waiting” after which “there will be no way to stop the transformation”.

But there is a difficulty in comparing a tipping point in, say, the melting of an ice sheet with a societal one. The first situation is mostly deterministic and irreversible once the system has passed a specific point; even the most extensive geoengineering scheme would struggle to stop a collapse once under way. By contrast, societal predictions are subject to the messy complexities of human agency. Potential societal and technological revolutions can and have been thwarted in the past. It is easy with hindsight to see societal transformations as inevitable and presaged by early warnings, but we run the risk of falling prey to a form of survivorship bias by failing to recognize all the shifts that didn’t happen and losing sight of the many other futures that might have been possible.
articles.reasoning: The article discusses a new book that applies the concept of tipping points from past societal transformations to current global challenges, including climate change and social inequality. It offers a hopeful perspective that humanity can learn from history to steer towards a sustainable future rather than a dystopia. The focus on environmental tipping points and societal change has broad significance for global society and the environment, providing meaningful insights and potential pathways for positive change.
articles.personality_title: Learning from history to guide society toward a sustainable future
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Societies in the past have gone through major changes called tipping points, where small shifts lead to big, lasting transformations. Today, the world faces problems like climate change and social inequality that could also cause tipping points.

**What happened** – Marten Scheffer, a researcher, wrote a new book called *Tipping Out of Trouble*. He studies past events like the collapse of ancient civilizations, the end of the slave trade, and social reforms to understand how societies changed quickly and deeply. Scheffer compares these past changes to today’s challenges, suggesting we can learn how to avoid disaster and create a better future.

**Impact** – Scheffer’s book shows that tipping points are not just about nature but also about human society. He explains that some changes happen suddenly and keep going once they start. This idea helps us see that big positive changes are possible if people act in time. It also warns that waiting too long could lead to serious problems that are hard to fix.

**What’s next step** – The book encourages people and leaders to recognize early signs of trouble and take strong, smart actions to guide society toward sustainability. Researchers will likely continue studying how to spot and influence these tipping points to avoid negative outcomes and promote positive shifts.

**One-sentence takeaway** – By studying past tipping points, we can better understand how to change today’s society toward a more sustainable and fair future before it’s too late.
articles.personality_title_fr: Apprendre du passé pour guider la société vers un avenir durable
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les sociétés ont connu dans le passé des changements majeurs appelés points de basculement, où de petits changements entraînent de grandes transformations durables. Aujourd’hui, le monde fait face à des problèmes comme le changement climatique et les inégalités sociales qui pourraient aussi provoquer ces points de basculement.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Marten Scheffer, un chercheur, a écrit un nouveau livre intitulé *Tipping Out of Trouble*. Il étudie des événements passés comme l’effondrement des civilisations anciennes, la fin de la traite des esclaves et les réformes sociales pour comprendre comment les sociétés ont changé rapidement et profondément. Scheffer compare ces changements passés aux défis d’aujourd’hui, suggérant que nous pouvons apprendre à éviter les catastrophes et à construire un avenir meilleur.

**Impact** – Le livre montre que les points de basculement concernent non seulement la nature, mais aussi la société humaine. Il explique que certains changements se produisent soudainement et continuent une fois lancés. Cette idée aide à comprendre que de grands changements positifs sont possibles si l’on agit à temps. Cela met aussi en garde contre le risque d’attendre trop longtemps, ce qui pourrait entraîner des problèmes difficiles à résoudre.

**Prochaine étape** – Le livre encourage les gens et les dirigeants à reconnaître les premiers signes de problème et à agir de manière forte et intelligente pour orienter la société vers la durabilité. Les chercheurs continueront probablement à étudier comment repérer et influencer ces points de basculement pour éviter des résultats négatifs et favoriser des changements positifs.

**Une phrase clé** – En étudiant les points de basculement du passé, nous pouvons mieux comprendre comment transformer la société actuelle vers un avenir plus durable et équitable avant qu’il ne soit trop tard.
articles.personality_title_es: Aprender del pasado para guiar a la sociedad hacia un futuro sostenible
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En el pasado, las sociedades han experimentado grandes cambios llamados puntos de inflexión, donde pequeños cambios provocan transformaciones grandes y duraderas. Hoy, el mundo enfrenta problemas como el cambio climático y la desigualdad social que también podrían causar estos puntos de inflexión.

**Qué pasó** – Marten Scheffer, un investigador, escribió un nuevo libro llamado *Tipping Out of Trouble*. Estudia eventos pasados como el colapso de civilizaciones antiguas, el fin del comercio de esclavos y reformas sociales para entender cómo las sociedades cambiaron rápida y profundamente. Scheffer compara estos cambios del pasado con los desafíos actuales, sugiriendo que podemos aprender a evitar desastres y crear un futuro mejor.

**Impacto** – El libro muestra que los puntos de inflexión no solo afectan a la naturaleza, sino también a la sociedad humana. Explica que algunos cambios ocurren de repente y continúan una vez que comienzan. Esta idea ayuda a entender que grandes cambios positivos son posibles si se actúa a tiempo. También advierte que esperar demasiado puede causar problemas difíciles de resolver.

**Próximo paso** – El libro anima a personas y líderes a reconocer señales tempranas de problemas y a tomar acciones fuertes e inteligentes para guiar a la sociedad hacia la sostenibilidad. Los investigadores probablemente seguirán estudiando cómo detectar e influir en estos puntos de inflexión para evitar resultados negativos y fomentar cambios positivos.

**Una frase clave** – Al estudiar los puntos de inflexión del pasado, podemos entender mejor cómo cambiar la sociedad actual hacia un futuro más sostenible y justo antes de que sea demasiado tarde.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of an ancient cliff dwelling gently transforming into a vibrant, interconnected network of glowing threads and blossoming seeds, symbolizing societal tipping points and positive transformation, rendered in warm earthy tones and soft natural colors with abstract shapes suggesting resilience and renewal.
